questions.question: Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM) is defined as cardiac dilatation with an end-diastolic diameter greater than:
questions.detail: DCM is defined by dilatation with an end-diastolic diameter greater than 55 mm.

questions.question: Which finding on Cardiac MRI is characteristic of the 'idiopathic' variety of Dilated Cardiomyopathy?
questions.detail: The idiopathic variety of DCM typically shows either no enhancement or linear mid-myocardial enhancement.

questions.question: In cases of DCM, there is often an associated valvular pathology due to ring dilation. Which valve is most commonly affected?
questions.detail: Dilation of the mitral ring in DCM commonly leads to mitral regurgitation.

questions.question: Restrictive Cardiomyopathy is primarily characterized by a decrease in:
questions.detail: Restrictive cardiomyopathy is fundamentally characterized by decreased diastolic function.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of Restrictive Cardiomyopathy according to the text?
questions.detail: Amyloidosis is identified as the most common cause of restrictive cardiomyopathy.

questions.question: Which condition is associated with biatrial enlargement, concentric thickening of the LV, and reduced systolic function of both ventricles?
questions.detail: Cardiac amyloidosis causes these changes, often featuring biatrial enlargement.

questions.question: On Cardiac MRI, what specific technical parameter must be adjusted to properly evaluate Cardiac Amyloidosis?
questions.detail: A long Inversion Time (around 350 ms compared to 200 ms normally) is necessary for evaluating amyloid.

questions.question: In Amyloidosis, the myocardium is described by which high-yield buzzword on MRI?
questions.detail: 'Difficult to suppress' is a specific buzzword for the amyloid myocardium on MRI.

questions.question: In a patient with Cardiac Amyloidosis, what is the expected appearance of the blood pool on delayed enhancement images when the TI is set correctly?
questions.detail: With a long TI, the blood pool in Amyloidosis may appear darker than the myocardium.

questions.question: Which type of Amyloid is associated with the buzzword 'Abnormal urinary light chains (AL)'?
questions.detail: Primary amyloid is linked to the 'Abnormal urinary light chains (AL)' buzzword.

questions.question: Eosinophilic Cardiomyopathy (Loeffler) is classically associated with which finding?
questions.detail: Bilateral ventricular thrombus is the classic phrase associated with Loeffler cardiomyopathy.

questions.question: To visualize the thrombus in Loeffler cardiomyopathy on MRI, what adjustment is often necessary?
questions.detail: A long TI is required to properly visualize the thrombus in Eosinophilic (Loeffler) CM.

questions.question: Constrictive Pericarditis is a pericardial process, whereas Restrictive Cardiomyopathy is a:
questions.detail: Restrictive CM is a myocardial process, while constrictive is a pericardial process.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of Constrictive Pericarditis in the developed world?
questions.detail: Post-CABG iatrogenic causes or viral infections are now the most common causes in the developed world.

questions.question: On CT, a pericardium is considered 'too thick' if it measures greater than:
questions.detail: The pericardium is considered abnormally thick if it measures > 0.4 cm.

questions.question: Which imaging finding is considered diagnostic for Constrictive Pericarditis on CT or CXR?
questions.detail: Pericardial calcification in the setting of a thick pericardium is diagnostic for constriction.

questions.question: 'Sigmoidization' of the septum and a 'Diastolic Bounce' on cine MRI are hallmarks of:
questions.detail: Septal 'sigmoidization' and a 'diastolic bounce' are characteristic MRI findings for constrictive disease.

questions.question: The 'Diastolic Bounce' in constrictive disease is most pronounced during which phase of respiration?
questions.detail: The bounce is most pronounced during inspiration because of ventricular interdependence.

questions.question: Myocarditis, often caused by Coxsackie virus, typically demonstrates Late Gadolinium Enhancement (LGE) in which distribution?
questions.detail: Late Gadolinium Enhancement in myocarditis is epicardial or mid-wall, rather than subendocardial.

questions.question: Which wall of the heart is the 'preferred' location for LGE in Myocarditis?
questions.detail: Late Gadolinium Enhancement in myocarditis prefers the lateral free wall.

questions.question: In Cardiac Sarcoidosis, which part of the heart is frequently involved?
questions.detail: Cardiac sarcoidosis commonly involves the intraventricular septum.

questions.question: How does Cardiac Sarcoidosis mimic Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy on imaging?
choices: Through focal wall thickening from edema
questions.detail: Sarcoidosis can mimic HCM through focal wall thickening caused by edema.

questions.question: 'Ballooning of the left ventricular apex' in a post-menopausal woman following a stressful event is the buzzword for:
questions.detail: This apical ballooning pattern in post-menopausal women is the classic description for Tako-Tsubo.

questions.question: What is the characteristic LGE pattern for Tako-Tsubo Cardiomyopathy?
questions.detail: Tako-Tsubo Cardiomyopathy is notable for having NO delayed enhancement.

questions.question: Which sequence is most useful for distinguishing between Acute and Chronic Myocardial Infarction?
choices: T2 (Acute is bright, Chronic is dark)
questions.detail: T2 weighted imaging helps distinguish acute (bright/edema) from chronic (dark/scar) MI.

questions.question: Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC) is characterized by which histological change?
choices: Fibrofatty replacement of the RV myocardium
questions.detail: Fibrofatty replacement of the RV myocardium is the defining histological change of ARVC.

questions.question: Which imaging sequence is 'watched out for' on tests to demonstrate fat in the RV wall in ARVC?
questions.detail: Fat saturation sequences are used on exams to demonstrate the fat in the RV wall for ARVC.

questions.question: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) results from abnormal hypertrophy caused by:
questions.detail: HCM is characterized by abnormal hypertrophy resulting from myofibril disarray.

questions.question: Which specific type of hypertrophy is typically shown on exams for HCM?
choices: Asymmetric hypertrophy of the intraventricular septum
questions.detail: Asymmetric hypertrophy of the intraventricular septum is the classic type of HCM shown on exams.

questions.question: 'Systolic Anterior Motion' (SAM) of the mitral valve in HCM is caused by:
choices: Venturi forces pulling the valve into the LVOT
questions.detail: SAM is caused by Venturi forces pulling the mitral valve into the LVOT during systole.

questions.question: In HCM, the presence of patchy midwall delayed enhancement in the hypertrophied muscle is an independent risk factor for:
questions.detail: Midwall enhancement in HCM serves as an independent risk factor for sudden death.

questions.question: Left Ventricular Noncompaction is a congenital cardiomyopathy characterized by:
choices: Loosely packed myocardium with a spongy appearance
questions.detail: Noncompaction is defined by a loosely packed myocardium with a spongy appearance.

questions.question: What is the diagnostic ratio for non-compacted to compacted end-diastolic myocardium in Noncompaction?
questions.detail: A ratio of more than 2.3:1 between non-compacted and compacted myocardium is diagnostic.

questions.question: What is the high-yield buzzword for Noncompaction?
questions.detail: 'Prominent Ventricular Trabeculation' is the high-yield buzzword for LV noncompaction.

questions.question: Becker and Duchenne muscular dystrophies are inherited in which fashion?
questions.detail: These muscular dystrophies are inherited in an X-linked fashion.

questions.question: The imaging hallmark of Muscular Dystrophy in the heart is a dilated heart with:
questions.detail: The hallmark is a dilated heart featuring mid-wall delayed enhancement.

questions.question: Jaffe-Campanacci Syndrome includes multiple Non-Ossifying Fibromas (NOFs) and which of the following?
questions.detail: Jaffe-Campanacci is a syndrome consisting of NOFs and cardiac malformations.

questions.question: Which syndrome combines polyostotic fibrous dysplasia and multiple soft tissue myxomas?
questions.detail: Mazabraud Syndrome is the combination of polyostotic fibrous dysplasia and multiple soft tissue myxomas.

questions.question: If you see 'tumors EVERYWHERE' in the head and neck (MSME), you should suspect:
questions.detail: The MSME (Multiple Schwannomas, Meningiomas, Ependymomas) triad signifies NF-2.

questions.question: Klinefelter Syndrome (47 XXY) is associated with a 300x increased risk for which thoracic pathology?
questions.detail: Klinefelter patients have a 300-fold increased risk for extragonadal germ cell tumors in the chest.

questions.question: Metastatic disease to the heart and pericardium is approximately how much more common than primary cardiac tumors?
questions.detail: Cardiac metastases are 30 times more common than primary tumors of the heart.

questions.question: Which site is 'by far' the most common affected by cardiac metastases?
questions.detail: The pericardium is the most common cardiac site for metastatic involvement.

questions.question: What is the most common manifestation of metastatic disease to the heart?
questions.detail: Pericardial effusion is the most frequent manifestation of cardiac metastases.

questions.question: Statistically, which cancer is the most common primary to metastasize to the heart?
questions.detail: Lung cancer is the most common primary source due to its high overall prevalence.

questions.question: Which cancer has the highest percentage of cases that involve the heart, despite having a lower overall prevalence than lung cancer?
questions.detail: Melanoma has the highest percentage of cases that involve the heart among cancers.

questions.question: What is the most common primary malignant tumor of the heart in adults?
questions.detail: Angiosarcoma is the most common primary malignant cardiac tumor in adults.

questions.question: Angiosarcomas classically favor which cardiac chamber?
questions.detail: Angiosarcomas classically favor the Right Atrium.

questions.question: The 'sun-ray' appearance on MRI, representing enhancement along perivascular spaces of epicardial vessels, is the buzzword for:
questions.detail: The 'sun-ray' appearance describes the enhancement of the diffuse subtype of angiosarcoma.

questions.question: What is the most common primary cardiac tumor in adults (typically benign)?
questions.detail: Myxoma is the most common adult primary cardiac tumor.

questions.question: Left Atrial Myxomas are characteristically attached to which structure?
questions.detail: Myxomas arise from the interatrial septum, typically near the fossa ovalis.

questions.question: Carney Complex is associated with which cardiac tumor?
questions.detail: Carney Complex is associated with cardiac myxomas and skin pigmentation.

questions.question: What is the 'important discriminator' to distinguish a cardiac tumor from a thrombus on MRI?
choices: Location (thrombus is only in the appendage)
Enhancement with Gadolinium (tumors enhance, thrombus does not)
questions.detail: Tumors enhance with gadolinium while thrombi do not.

questions.question: Which tumor is the most common fetal cardiac tumor and is considered a hamartoma?
questions.detail: Rhabdomyoma is a hamartoma and the most common fetal cardiac tumor.

questions.question: Cardiac Rhabdomyomas have a very strong (and testable) association with:
questions.detail: Rhabdomyomas are strongly associated with Tuberous Sclerosis.

questions.question: What is the typical clinical course for Rhabdomyomas associated with Tuberous Sclerosis?
questions.detail: Most of these tumors undergo spontaneous regression.

questions.question: Which tumor is the second most common cardiac tumor in childhood and favors the interventricular septum?
questions.detail: Fibroma is the second most common childhood cardiac tumor and favors the IV septum.

questions.question: How do Cardiac Fibromas typically appear on T1 and T2 weighted MRI?
questions.detail: Cardiac fibromas are dark on both T1 and T2 weighted MRI.

questions.question: What is the most common neoplasm to involve the cardiac valves?
questions.detail: Fibroelastoma is the most common neoplasm involving the heart valves.

questions.question: Fibroelastomas are described as being 'highly mobile' on which sequence?
questions.detail: Fibroelastomas are described as being highly mobile on SSFP Cine imaging.

questions.question: To distinguish a Fibroelastoma from a vegetation, one should know that vegetations tend to involve the:
choices: Aortic side of the cusp only
questions.detail: Vegetations involve the valve free edges, whereas fibroelastomas do not.

questions.question: The normal pericardium contains approximately how much fluid?
questions.detail: Normally, there is about 50 cc of fluid between the pericardial layers.

questions.question: A 'pericardial effusion' is defined when the fluid volume between the visceral and parietal layers exceeds:
questions.detail: An effusion is defined when the volume of fluid exceeds 50 cc.

questions.question: According to the text, which condition is a common cause of uremic pericardial effusion?
questions.detail: Renal failure (uremia) is identified as a common cause of pericardial effusion.

questions.question: For multiple choice tests, which autoimmune disease should you associate with an inflammatory pericardial effusion?
questions.detail: SLE is the 'for the purpose of multiple choice' autoimmune cause for inflammatory effusions.

questions.question: 'Dressler Syndrome' refers to an inflammatory pericardial effusion occurring after:
questions.detail: Dressler syndrome is an inflammatory effusion occurring post-MI.

questions.question: On a frontal chest radiograph, a massive pericardial effusion is often described as having which shape?
questions.detail: The 'Giant Water Bottle heart' is the classic CXR description for massive pericardial effusion.

questions.question: The 'Oreo Cookie Sign' on a lateral chest X-ray consists of a central opaque line (fluid) between two lucent lines of:
questions.detail: The sign is formed by two lucent fat lines with central opaque fluid.

questions.question: Cardiac Tamponade occurs when pericardial pressure compromises the filling of chambers, starting with the:
questions.detail: Tamponade compromises the filling of the atria first, then the ventricles.

questions.question: Tamponade can occur with as little as 100 cc of fluid if:
choices: The rate of accumulation is very fast
questions.detail: The rate of accumulation is key; even 100 cc can cause tamponade if it accumulates quickly.

questions.question: On short-axis MRI during deep inspiration, a sign of tamponade is the flattening or inversion of the:
questions.detail: Inversion of the septum toward the LV during deep inspiration is a sign of tamponade.

questions.question: An indirect sign of Cardiac Tamponade that can be seen on CT is the reflux of contrast into the:
questions.detail: Reflux of contrast into the IVC and azygos system is an indirect CT sign of tamponade.

questions.question: Pericardial cysts are benign incidental findings most commonly located at the:
questions.detail: The right anterior cardiophrenic angle is the classic location for pericardial cysts.

questions.question: What is the typical imaging characteristic of a pericardial cyst on CT?
questions.detail: On CT, pericardial cysts characteristically measure as water density.

questions.question: Regarding the pericardium, do pericardial cysts communicate with the pericardial space?
questions.detail: Pericardial cysts are benign incidental findings that do not communicate with the pericardium.

questions.question: What is the most common clinical scenario for partial absence of the pericardium?
choices: Absence over the Left Atrium and adjacent pulmonary artery
Absence of the entire parietal layer
Absence only in the posterior mediastinum
It is always a total absence
questions.detail: The most common scenario is absence over the Left Atrium and the adjacent pulmonary artery.

questions.question: When the left pericardium is absent, the heart typically shifts toward the:
questions.detail: The heart typically shifts toward the left when the left pericardium is absent.

questions.question: Which structure is 'most at risk for strangulation' in the setting of partial pericardial absence?
questions.detail: The left atrial appendage is the structure most at risk for strangulation.

questions.question: Cardiac herniation and volvulus can occur following which surgical procedure?
questions.detail: Herniation can occur after extrapleural pneumonectomy.

questions.question: Pericardial calcification that is largest over the AV groove is a diagnostic finding for:
questions.detail: Calcification over the AV groove is characteristic for constrictive pericarditis.

questions.question: For 'gamesmanship,' if you see a 30-year-old female with an ugly rash on her face and a 'Really Big Heart' on CXR, you should suspect:
questions.detail: This combination suggests an SLE-related pericardial effusion.

questions.question: The Bentall Procedure involves a composite graft replacement of the aortic valve, aortic root, and ascending aorta, along with:
choices: Re-implantation of the coronary arteries into the graft
questions.detail: Coronary arteries must be re-implanted into the graft during a Bentall procedure.

questions.question: The Bentall Procedure is classically used to treat lesions associated with which syndrome?
questions.detail: The procedure is classically used for aortic root and valve disease in Marfan syndrome.

questions.question: The Glenn Shunt is a 'Vein to Artery' procedure that connects the:
choices: Subclavian artery to the pulmonary artery
Left atrium to the right atrium
questions.detail: The Glenn shunt connects the SVC to the pulmonary artery.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Glenn shunt?
choices: Increase pulmonary blood flow by bypassing the right heart
Provide blood to a transplanted heart
questions.detail: The primary purpose is to increase pulmonary blood flow by bypassing the right heart.

questions.question: A 'testable complication' of the Glenn procedure is the development of:
choices: SVC Syndrome or Pulmonary Artery Aneurysms
questions.detail: SVC Syndrome and PA Aneurysms are testable complications of the Glenn procedure.

questions.question: The Blalock-Taussig (BT) Shunt is an 'Artery to Artery' procedure connecting the:
choices: Subclavian artery to the pulmonary artery
questions.detail: The BT shunt connects the subclavian artery to the pulmonary artery.

questions.question: The primary purpose of a Blalock-Taussig shunt is to:
questions.detail: The primary purpose of the BT shunt is to increase pulmonary blood flow.

questions.question: A major testable complication of the Blalock-Taussig shunt is:
choices: Stenosis at the shunt’s pulmonary insertion site
questions.detail: Stenosis at the insertion site is a major testable complication.

questions.question: The Fontan Procedure is used to:
choices: Bypass the right ventricle by directing systemic circulation into the PAs
Replace a diseased aortic valve in kids
questions.detail: The Fontan procedure bypasses the RV by directing systemic circulation into the PAs.

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'unique and testable' complication of the Fontan procedure?
questions.detail: Plastic Bronchitis is a unique and testable Fontan complication.

questions.question: Another complication of the Fontan procedure is arrhythmia caused by an:
questions.detail: RA enlargement after Fontan can cause arrhythmia.

questions.question: In an Orthotopic Heart Transplant:
choices: All of the heart is removed except the circular part of the left atrium
The recipient heart remains in place and the donor heart is added on top
It gives the native heart a chance to recover
questions.detail: In orthotopic transplant, the whole heart is removed except for part of the left atrium.

questions.question: A Heterotopic Heart Transplant:
choices: Involves removing the whole native heart
Adds a donor heart on top of the native heart
Is the most common type of transplant
questions.detail: Heterotopic transplant ('double heart') adds a donor heart on top of the native heart.

questions.question: The 'Ross Procedure' replaces a diseased aortic valve with which structure?
questions.detail: Replacing the aortic valve with the patient's own pulmonary valve is the Ross procedure.

questions.question: For whom is the Ross Procedure typically performed?
questions.detail: The Ross procedure is typically done for diseased aortic valves in children.

questions.question: The Jatene Procedure is a type of arterial switch used to treat:
questions.detail: The Jatene switch is used for D-Transposition.

questions.question: The 'LeCompte Maneuver,' where the PA is draped over the aorta, is part of which procedure?
questions.detail: The LeCompte maneuver is a high-yield buzzword for the Jatene arterial switch.

questions.question: The Rastelli Procedure involves a 'RV Baffle' and is indicated for:
questions.detail: The Rastelli RV Baffle is indicated for transposition with pulmonary outflow obstruction.

questions.question: Pulmonary Artery Banding is used to treat:
choices: CHF in infancy or Single Ventricle
questions.detail: P-Artery Banding is used to manage flow in infancy CHF or Single Ventricle.

questions.question: 'Arterial Switch' procedures like the Senning and Mustard were used for:
questions.detail: Senning and Mustard switches are used for Transposition of the Great Arteries.
